text: Compounds other than fluorides and chlorides have been suggested, including phosphates, $^{3}$ sulfates, $^{4}$ sulfides, $^{4}$ hydrosulfides, $^{4}$ and hydroxides. $^{5,6}$ Molten fluoride mixtures, e.g.

LiF-NaF, have the most desirable properties: they dissolve adequate amounts of fuel; they have satisfactory heat transfer properties; they resist radiation; they can tolerate an accumulation of fission products; the melting points are low enough that corrosion problems of excessively high temperatures are avoided; and the vapor pressures are low enough to permit low-pressure operation. $^{4,7,8}$ Although fluorine itself has some moderating properties, a better moderator must be present, either in the molten-salt mixture or as a separate structure, to obtain a thermal reactor of reasonable size.

Molten-salt reactors are attractive concepts for several reasons. $^{1,2,7}$ They provide high temperatures in a low-pressure system to produce steam at temperatures high enough to give high thermal-cycle efficiencies.
According to Weinberg, $^{10}$ a great advantage is that the fissionable materials can be consumed at very high thermal efficiencies and with extremely high burnup.

In 1959, the Fluid Fuel Reactor Task Force compared the aqueous homogeneous, molten-salt, and liquid-metal fueled reactors.[11] The principal conclusions were: the molten-salt reactor had the best chance of achieving technical feasibility; only with the homogeneous aqueous reactor was there a possibility of achieving a reasonably short doubling time; and the total power costs for the MSR were between those for the aqueous reactor and the liquid-metal-fueled reactor.
MacPherson, and Frank Maslan, Fluid Fuel Reactors, Addison Wesley Publishing Co., Reading Mass., 1958.
Part I, NAA-SR-5925, Atomics International, Sept.
Clegg, Fused Salt Mixtures as Potential Liquid Fuels for Nuclear Power Reactors, BMI-864, Del., Battelle Memorial Institute, Sept.
Estabrook, Circulating Potassium Hydroxide Reactors, Y-F8-9, Del., ORNL, Dec.
Ritzmann, Molten-Salt Reactors: Report for 1960 Ten-Year-Plan Evaluation, Unpublished report, ORNL, July 25, 1960.
Conf. on Peaceful Uses of Atomic Energy, Geneva, 1958, 28, pp.
99-111, United Nations, New York, 1958.
Quarterly Progress Report for Period Ending December 31, 1957, A.
Miller, Project Coordinator, ORNL-2440, Del., ORNL, April 24, 1958.
Grimes, Molten Salts as Reactor Materials, Nuclear News 7, No.
In 1957, however, work on circulating-fuel reactors for aircraft was discontinued.
The MSRE (Molten Salt Reactor Experiment) is the current major effort on this type of reactor, with criticality achieved in mid 1965.
Ferguson in 1950 and 1951 included both reactors fueled with molten fluorides and a few fueled with suspensions of uranium oxide in molten sodium hydroxide.

In the Homogeneous Circulating Suspension Reactor, the fuel is a suspension of 2.2 wt. $\%$ uranium oxide in sodium hydroxide, which acts as moderator. $^{1}$ The fuel enters the top of the reactor, where a whirling motion is imparted to it by vanes, and leaves through the bottom to a heat exchanger.
The design power for this reactor is 140 MW(t).

In 1952, Dayton and Chastain made calculations for the design of one- and two-region circulating reactors using hydroxides as moderator-coolants.4 The compounds studied were sodium hydroxide, lithium-7 hydroxide, and lithium-7 deuteroxide.
Uranium-233 oxide, suspended in the hydroxide within the spherical reactor, was the fuel specified.
If, however, cost and size are not considered, Li $^{7}$ OD appears to be the most attractive of the compounds.
Two ORNL designs were for a 200-MW(t) Aircraft Reactor (1951) $^{5}$ and a Circulating-fuel Reactor for Direct Heat Transfer to Engines (1953). $^{6}$

The ARE was built at Oak Ridge National Laboratory as a circulating-fuel reactor of low power and high temperature, but materials suitable for use in a reactor of high power were employed.[7-9] According to Weinberg, "The purpose of this reactor experiment was simply to gain experience in handling salts in a reactor at very high temperatures, to see whether one could in fact contain the intensely radioactive circulating fuel, and to study the kinetic behavior of the reactor."[10]
Stagnant fluoride fuel was used in the first design.[11] The core would have a cylindrical moderator matrix, containing vertical holes into which small fuel tubes would be placed.
The maximum design power was 2.5 MW(t).[12,13] The reactor became critical on November 11, 1954, and it was shut down the following evening.
It had demonstrated the feasibility of using a high-temperature fluoride fuel in a circulating-fuel reactor.[14]

A reactor with a tandem heat exchanger was suggested as a modification of the ARE.[15] The moderator-reflector is water or sodium hydroxide, with the core and the tandem heat exchanger being surrounded by a layer of water at 300-350 psi.

A concept that originated before the operation of the ARE and was scheduled for use in later developments was a circulating-fuel, reflectormoderated reactor, known originally as the Fireball. $^{17,18}$ In this reactor there are no fuel tubes.

In the earliest design the central island was spherical, but later development resulted in a vase-shaped island. $^{18}$ This shape, according to Fraas, reduces the critical mass, improves power distribution, and hydrodynamically gives the best passage for fuel. $^{18}$ Cooling is by circulating the fluid salt to a circumferential wraparound heat exchanger between the moderator shell and the pressure shell.
Fraas and Savolainen have discussed eight core designs for the spherical reflector-moderated reactor, with a wraparound heat exchanger, that are related to the Fireball.[19]

Some concepts that have employed the Fireball design with little change are the Aircraft Reactor Test,[20] The Circulating Fluoride-fuel High Flux Reactor,[21] and a modified Fireball concept of General Electric.[22] In the High-flux Reactor, which includes a central island of graphite, use of a layer of bismuth between the central island and the fuel layer was suggested by the author to resist flow of thermal neutrons from the central island, where they are created, to the shell, where they are absorbed.

Pratt & Whitney concepts were originally variations and developments of the Fireball design, with changes in the dimensions and other factors to give reactors of different power levels.[23,24] In two simplified versions[25] the annulus of the Fireball is replaced by five tubes in the center of the core.

The beryllium-moderated concept was developed into a more complete design.[26] In another modification, the island is cylindrical, rather than vase shaped as in the original Fireball.[27] Other variations considered briefly were the use of beryllium, beryllium oxide, or graphite for the island and for the reflector, and using and not using a reflector.

Alternative fuels considered were lithium or beryllium fluorides as bases for fuels, and slurries of uranium dioxides in alkali metals.[24] Lithium-7 was considered as a coolant,[24] and zirconium hydride as a moderator.
The use of zirconium hydride as a moderator was incorporated in one concept.[28] The core consists of fuel tubes and zirconium hydride rods, with lithium-7 flowing parallel to the tubes.
Lithium-7 could circulate directly to the engine radiator.

An ORSORT concept, the Screwball (1953), differed from the Fireball chiefly in that helical fuel tubes replaced the annular passage between the central island, which was eliminated, and the moderator shell.30 Thus it somewhat resembles the Pratt & Whitney concept described in Ref.
Lower power density $(2.5\mathrm{kW / cm}^3)$ was chosen to aid problems of questionable fuel density.

ORSORT students designed a molten-salt reactor for ship propulsion, which contained many of the features of the aircraft reactor concepts.31 A compact, high-performance reactor was sought in order to reduce weight.
This 125-MW(t) reactor has a nickel reflector, as well as extensive shielding.

An early concept, on which little has been published, is for a thorium converter for electrical power production, which was developed by Davidson and Robb.[32] In this converter, the fuel is a solution of 93 percent enriched $\mathrm{U}^{235}\mathrm{F}_4$ in molten fluorides containing thorium fluoride for conversion.

The Fused Salt Reactor for Power and Heat, proposed in 1953, could be either a burner or converter, with thorium fluoride added to the molten salt if conversion is desired.[33] The core is a graphite sphere, through which fuel circulates from the bottom through channels cut into the graphite.
The reactor is designed for installation at remote locations to produce power--about 5 MW(t)--and heat.
In the Fused Salt Breeder Reactor (FSBR),<sup>34</sup> the fuel is a solution of uranium-233 fluoride and thorium fluoride in fused lithium-7 fluoride and beryllium fluoride.
Two sizes were considered, one for 125 MW(e) and one for 250 MW(e).
The 600-MW Fused Salt Homogeneous Reactor Power Plant<sup>35</sup> utilizes a fuel salt of fluorides of uranium-235, zirconium, and sodium.
The design of this burner could be altered so as to breed uranium-235.
The Fused Salt Reactor for Process Heat (1956) $^{36}$ has two features that differ somewhat from previous designs.
Of the 400 MW(t) produced by the reactor, 35 Mw is generated in the MgO moderator and is used to heat steam from heat exchangers.
The use of natural convection has been suggested to eliminate the problem of providing reliable, long-lived pumps for fuel circulation.[37,38] This advantage is at the cost of a greater fuel volume.
Either would give a power of 22 MW(e).
Molten lead is suggested as coolant in an ORNL concept of 1958.[39] The only moderator is the fluorine in the fuel salt--NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ .
The design power is 194 MW(e).
Two that were developed furthest were the Slightly Enriched, Fused-Salt-Fueled Reactor and the Experimental Molten-Salt-Fueled 30 MW(t) Power Reactor.
The Slightly Enriched, Fused-Salt-Fueled Reactor $^{40-42}$ is a converter fueled with slightly enriched uranium tetrafluoride dissolved in molten lithium fluoride and beryllium fluoride.
It was proposed in 1958, and a preliminary design was published in 1959.
The reactor produces 315 MW(e).
The Experimental Molten-Salt-Fueled 30-MW(t) Power Reactor $^{42,43}$ is a burner fueled with highly enriched uranium tetrafluoride dissolved in molten lithium fluoride and beryllium fluoride.
The core is a sphere of INOR-8.

The Molten Salt Reactor Experiment $^{44-46}$ is the first of the three stages in the development of molten-salt reactors discussed by MacPherson in 1960. $^{47}$ After this one-region reactor, a two-region reactor experiment was planned, and a high-power prototype of a molten-salt reactor would follow.

The use of molten fluorides as fuel in a 10-MW(e) fast reactor for spacecraft was considered by Allen.[48] Calculations were made to estimate the size and weight of such a reactor and to compare them with those of a reactor fueled with uranium carbide.
Highly enriched (93.5 percent) uranium tetrafluoride was the fuel, with fluorides of sodium, beryllium, lithium, and zirconium considered as solvents.
The fuel mixture for which calculations were made is 70 percent $\mathrm{UF}_4$ , 30 percent NaF.

Construction is in progress, and criticality was achieved in 1965.[49] The one-region reactor is meant as an intermediate step in developing a large two-region breeder, but one-region reactors have also been suggested for uses in their own right, especially in smaller power stations.[47]

Maximum power: $2.5\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

section: No. 35 Reflector-moderated, Circulating-fuel Aircraft Reactor (Screwball) ORSORT
text: Power: $200\mathrm{MW(t)}$
Status: Design and feasibility study, 1957.
Fuel-coolant: $U^{235}$ in molten-salt mixture containing 49 mol $\%$ NaF, $45\%$ ZrF $_4$ , $6\%$ UF $_4$ .
Rods tipped with poison material (BeO plus B $^{10}$ ) to reduce end leakage, as well as to reduce fissioning in exit and entrance plenums for fuel.
Reflector: Ni blanket, 6 in. thick, around core.
It is surrounded by $5\frac{1}{2}$ -in. thick region of cylindrical rods, $3/4$ -in. thick, containing mixture of BeO and B $^{10}$ .
Reactor vessel: cylinder, 80 by 80 in., containing expansion tank for fuel and coolant coils in head for removing internally generated heat by flow of portion of fuel.
Shielding: primary--structural steel plus 5 in.
H $_2$ O; secondary--4-6 $\frac{1}{2}$ in.

Fuel flows up through central core region (75 cm diameter, 80 cm high) and down through annular downcomer at periphery containing primary (fuel-to-secondary fluid) wraparound heat exchanger cooled by molten salt (30 mol $\%$ NaF, $20\%$ LiF, $50\%$ BeF $_2$ ).

Control: negative temperature coefficient; varying coolant flow; single vertical control rod (Inconel-BeO-Ni-1 vol. $\%$ B $^{10}$ ) in thimble extending length of core at core centerline for reactor shutdown, change in mean temperature, and fuel burnup; rod thimble about 4 in. diameter, with gap for cooling by molten salt or metal; provision for emergency fuel dumping.

section: No. 38 Molten-salt Thorium Converter for Electrical Power Production Knolls Atomic Power Laboratory
text: Up to $85\%$ average conversion of Th232 to U233 is possible; conversion ratio would be 0.73 to 0.905.
Critical mass $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ : 2.7 kg.
No provisions for cooling channels in graphite moderator because its temperature will not exceed $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at maximum power level and with surface cooling.
Fuel-coolant-fertile material: solution of $\mathbf{U}^{233}\mathbf{F}_4$ and $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ in molten Li $^7$ F and $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ .
Critical radius of core: 160 cm.
Average operating temperature: $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Two sizes of reactors: 310 MW(t), 125 MW(e); 616 MW(t), 250 MW(e).
Fuel-coolant: solution of highly enriched $\mathrm{U}^{235}\mathrm{F}_4$ in molten NaF and ZrF.
Core: cylinder, 2 ft diameter by 10 ft high, surrounded by tube bundles of primary heat exchanger.
Inlet temperature: $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; outlet: $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Reactor designed to burn $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ , but design could be altered to breed $\mathrm{U}^{233}$ .
Fuel-coolant: $93\%$ enriched $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ in NaF-ZrF₄.
Fuel circulated at 17.4 ft/sec through 90 2-in.
ID Inconel tubes in triangular array on 12-in. centers.
Tubes are in a 10-ft-diameter by 12-ft-high cylindrical matrix of hexagonally shaped blocks of MgO.

Moderator perforated to allow passage of steam through tubes inserted in holes. $35\mathrm{MW(t)}$ is generated in the MgO and used to heat steam from $2040^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $3000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Fuel heated from $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a single pass upward through the core by internal heat generation; delivers the heat to Na in an external heat exchanger.
Core reflected on sides with 8-in.
BeO and contained in a 12-ft-diameter by 18-ft-long steel pressure vessel.
Design includes 4 such reactors for an integrated coal-hydrogenation plant.
Each produces $400\mathrm{MW(t)}$ ; $123\mathrm{MW(e)}$ .

section: No. 43 Molten Salt Natural Convection Reactor American Standard and ORNL
text: Core vessel: sphere, 8 ft diameter.

section: No. 43 Molten Salt Natural Convection Reactor American Standard and ORNL
text: Maximum fuel temperature: $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; minimum: 975-1025°F.

section: No. 43 Molten Salt Natural Convection Reactor American Standard and ORNL
text: Power: 60 MW(t); 22 MW(e).

section: No. 44 Homogeneous Fused Salt Power Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel-moderator: highly enriched $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ dissolved in molten NaF- $\mathbf{ZrF}_4$ (57 mol $\%$ -43 mol $\%$ ).

section: No. 44 Homogeneous Fused Salt Power Reactor ORNL
text: Core: cylinder, 10 ft diameter, 10 ft high.

section: No. 44 Homogeneous Fused Salt Power Reactor ORNL
text: Power: 600 MW(t); 194 MW(e).

section: No. 45 Slightly Enriched, Fused-salt-fueled Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel-coolant: slightly-enriched 1.3- $1.8\%$ $\mathrm{U}^{235}\mathrm{F}_4$ in molten LiF-BeF $_2$ .

section: No. 45 Slightly Enriched, Fused-salt-fueled Reactor ORNL
text: Unclad graphite moderator core, $12\frac{1}{4}$ ft in diameter and height, is contained in a cylindrical INOR-8 vessel.

section: No. 45 Slightly Enriched, Fused-salt-fueled Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel flows at 35,470 gpm from bottom to top of core through 3.6 in. holes on 8-in. centers in the graphite.

section: No. 45 Slightly Enriched, Fused-salt-fueled Reactor ORNL
text: Initial conversion ratio of U to Pu: about 0.79.

section: No. 45 Slightly Enriched, Fused-salt-fueled Reactor ORNL
text: Power: about 775 MW(t); 315 MW(e).

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel-moderator-coolant: solution of $90\%$ enriched $\mathrm{U}^{235}\mathrm{F}_4$ in molten LiF-BeF $_2$ .

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Core: sphere, 6 ft diameter.

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel circulates at 1480 gpm through bottom of core, flows upward at walls of core to heat exchanger and back to core, where it flows down.

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Steam produced at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 1450 psi.

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Exit temperature of fuel: $1235^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Structural material: INOR-8.

section: No. 46 Experimental Molten-salt-fueled $30\mathrm{MW(t)}$ Power Reactor ORNL
text: Power: 30 MW(t); 10 MW(e).
Fuel-coolant: solution of $93.5\%$ enriched $\mathrm{U}^{235}\mathrm{F}_4$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ .
Intermediate coolant: $\mathrm{Li}^7\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .

Moderator: unclad graphite, 1064 stringers (2 x 2 x 63 in. long) loosely pinned to restraining beams at core bottom; form cylindrical core (about 5 ft diameter by 7.5 ft high) contained in a reactor vessel.

Flow is laminar at 1200 gpm; fuel, blanketed by helium gas, enters top of core at $635^\circ\mathrm{C}$ , flows in a spiral path downward in annulus along the wall to the bottom where a dished head reverses the flow.
All components in contact with the fuel are of INOR-8.
Exiting at $663^\circ\mathrm{C}$ the fuel enters the sump-type pump from which it is discharged through the shell side of the heat exchanger back to the core inlet.
Control: negative temperature coefficient; 3 shim control rods--thin-walled cylinders of $\mathrm{B}_4\mathrm{C}$ made of stacked short sections clad in INOR-8.
Fuel: uranium as $93.5\%$ enriched $\mathrm{UF}_4$ dissolved in molten-salt mixture-- $70\%$ $\mathrm{UF}_4$ , $30\%$ NaF.
Core: sphere, 21 in. diameter, 40 liters vol.
Core composition: $42\%$ $\mathrm{UF}_4$ , $18\%$ NaF, and $10\%$ Zr by volume.
Reflector: internal, Zr; external, 3 in.

A problem with this structure is that it is unlikely that all of the 10,000 tubes needed for a 200 MW(e) reactor would keep their integrity for a long reactor lifetime.

In considering hydroxide-moderated two-region reactors, Dayton and Chastain emphasized lithium-7 hydroxide and lithium-7 deuteroxide.2 The spherical reactor, consisting of concentric shells of zirconium, has an outer annulus, which contains a suspension of thorium oxide in heavy water.
The specified core temperature of $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was assumed to be high enough for efficient power production but low enough to avoid excessive corrosion.
Breeding gains greater than 0.2 were postulated for reactors with either lithium compound.
Li $^{7}$ OD would be preferable.
The reactor moderated with Li $^{7}$ OH has a lower-cost moderator, smaller core radius, and smaller fuel requirements.
These advantages, however, are more than offset by those of the reactor moderated with Li $^{7}$ OD.
Also, although more fuel is required, it is a much smaller percentage of the total mass than is required with the reactor moderated with Li $^{7}$ OH.
A reactor designed in early 1952 that differed from most two-region reactors was the MIT Fluid Fuel Fused-salt Reactor reported by Goodman et al.
A similar design for a fast reactor was employed by Bulmer, et al., students at the Oak Ridge School of Reactor Technology, in 1956.
A power of $260\mathrm{MW(e)}$ was given.
This design was for a reactor of high power--1563 MW(t).

Zasler has proposed a reactor, $^{7,8}$ for which few details are given, in which circulation of fuel could be either by natural convection for a 5-MW experimental reactor, or by fuel pumps for a 50-MW(t) pilot plant.

This ORNL concept $^{9-11}$ has been given many names: MSR; Interim Design Reactor; Molten Fluoride Converter; Molten Fluoride Power Reactor; Reference Design Reactor; RDR; MSR Reference Design; and Homogeneous, Two-region, Molten-fluoride-salt Reactor.
Plutonium-239 has been suggested as an alternative fuel. $^{12}$ A fertile blanket of the same composition as the fuel salt but without the uranium surrounds the fuel region.
The reactor, designed for central-station power, would produce 260 MW(e).

Members of the MSR Project staff selected a typical design for calculation of reactor performance.[13] The core is a graphite shell structure with a single cylinder of graphite as the main part and two graphite end pieces.
The three pieces are held together by rods of INOR-8 or other alloy.
The fuel, uranium-233 in a molten fluoride carrier containing thorium, passes downward through channels in the core then back upward to an external heat exchanger.
The power would be 125 MW(t).

A reactor considered in 1961 in a comparison of homogeneous reactors for thorium breeding was the MSBR, $^{14,15}$ which was based on a design by MacPherson in 1959. $^{1}$ In both the 1959 and 1961 designs, the fuel is a solution of enriched uranium tetrafluoride in LiF-BeF $_2$ , the blanket surrounding the core is molten salt containing thorium, and the fuel passes through tubes in the graphite moderator.
In the 1959 design, the core is of the unit-fuel-tube construction, in which one-pass graphite tubes go through the graphite moderator.
In the 1961 design, the core is constructed of graphite prisms, machined at the corners to form vertical
The 1959 concept is for three reactors producing 333 MW(e) each, the 1961 concept for two reactors producing 500 MW(e) each.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Fuel-moderator-coolant: Homogeneous suspension of $\mathbf{U}^{233}\mathbf{O}_2$ in LiOH.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Minimum fuel for criticality: about 2.5 kg $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ .

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Reflector-breeding blanket: circulating suspension of $\mathrm{ThO_2}$ in $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$ , about $1\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ , in spherical annulus.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Blanket thickness: 60 cm.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Core contained by 2 concentric Zr shells, 0.5 cm thick.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Shells separated by air space 1 cm thick, which is thermal barrier.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Core temperature: $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Blanket temperature: $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide
text: Breeding gain: more than 0.2.

section: No. 1 Reflected Homogeneous Reactor Moderated by Lithium-7 Hydroxide

text: Fuel coolant: circulating mixture of molten NaCl, $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ , $\mathrm{PuCl}_3$ , and $\mathrm{U}^{238}\mathrm{Cl}_3$ ; $\mathrm{PuCl}_3$ is assumed to be in solution with $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ ; depleted U is for internal breeding.
Fuel contained in a 72.5 in.
ID, nearly spherical, vessel, tapered at the top and bottom to 24 in. for pipe connections.
Reflector: 1 in. liquid Pb immediately surrounding core.
Around reflector is blanket, under 100 psi pressure.
Stationary blanket is divided into 2 regions by a $5\frac{1}{8}$ -in. stainless steel-clad graphite moderator.
An 8 in. graphite reflector surrounds second blanket region.
Fuel enters reactor core at bottom at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaves through the top at $1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , then is pumped through an external loop and tube side of Na heat exchanger.
Estimated breeding ratio: 1.09.
Power: 700 MW(t), 260 MW(e).
Fuel: $\mathrm{U}^{233}\mathrm{F}_4$ in molten mixture-- ThF4-LiF-BeF2.
Two compositions: ThF4--7, 13%;
Doubling times: with 7 mol % Th²³², 27.5 years; with 13 mol %, 22.5 years.
Further reduction possible by different core arrangement: 2 concentric tubes, with fuel in the annulus and coolant flowing through the inner tube and on outside of outer tube.

section: No. 6 5 MW(50 MW) Thermal-convection Molten Salt Reactor
text: 6 5 MW(50 MW) Thermal-convection Molten Salt Reactor
Spherical core, 5 ft diameter, surrounded by 0.5 ft blanket.
Fuel-coolant: flows at 158 gpm through reactor and shell-and-tube heat exchanger, cooled by Na, entering the heat exchanger at $1210^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaving at $1010^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
5-MW(t) experimental reactor could be converted to 50-MW(t) pilot plant by adding fuel pumps (to increase flow to 1515 gpm) and increasing capacity of the fuel pumps.
Fuel-coolantmoderator: mixture of $90\%$ enriched $\mathbf{U}^{235}\mathbf{F}_4$ with LiF-BeF2-ThF4; alternate fuel: $\mathbf{Pu}^{239}$ instead of $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ .
Fertile material: 2-ft-thick blanket, of $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ as eutectic of $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ , completely surrounding fuel region.
Several core configurations considered: 1) straight-through flow, 2) concentric inlet (inner pipe)-outlet (outer annulus) flow, 3) concentric inlet (outer annulus)-outlet (inner pipe) flow.
Core: inverted-pear-shaped, Ni-Mo alloy, 8-ft in diameter (6-ft in early designs), surmounted by expansion chamber containing fuel pump.
Fuel temperature: approximately $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Blanket produces $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ at conversion ratio of 0.6 to 0.8, depending upon processing.
Reactor designed as central-station power plant to produce $600\mathrm{MW(t)}$ , $260\mathrm{MW(e)}$ .

section: No. 8 Two-region, Graphite-moderated Molten-salt Breeder Reactor ORNL
text: Fuel-coolant: $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ , presumably as $\mathbf{UF}_4$ , dissolved in molten LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ .

section: No. 8 Two-region, Graphite-moderated Molten-salt Breeder Reactor ORNL
text: Fertile material: 30-in. blanket of $13\mathrm{mol}\%$ Th in molten $\mathsf{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ .

section: No. 8 Two-region, Graphite-moderated Molten-salt Breeder Reactor ORNL
text: Core: main part made from single cylinder of graphite, about 5 ft diameter, 5 ft long, and two end pieces.

section: No. 8 Two-region, Graphite-moderated Molten-salt Breeder Reactor ORNL
text: Three pieces held together by rods of INOR-8 or other alloy.

section: No. 8 Two-region, Graphite-moderated Molten-salt Breeder Reactor ORNL
text: Parallel vertical channels for fuel flow, $1/2$ in. max. radius.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Core cylinder 7½-ft x 7½-ft. of graphite moderator prisms, 7.5 in. square by approx.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Corners machined to form vertical passages of about 5 in. diameter circular cross-section.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Fuel flows through 90 2-pass bayonet graphite tubes inserted into these vertical passages.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Core surrounded on all sides by 3-ft blanket, which is then surrounded by the 1-ft graphite reflector.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Maximum fuel temperature: 1300°F; minimum 1125°F.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Operating pressure: 100 psi.

section: No. 9 Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) ORNL
text: Power station would include 2 such reactors to produce 2364 MW(t) or 1000 MW(e)--1182 MW(t) and 500 MW(e) each.
Fuel-coolant: $\mathbf{U}^{235}\mathbf{F}_4$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ , at minimum temperature of $975^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and maximum of $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , circulating at 20 fps.
Core: 5.3 ft in diameter; blanket: 2.5 ft thick.
Possible breeding ratio: 1.06.
Each of 3 reactors in plant produces 815 MW(t), 333 MW(e).
